commit_message: Finalize technical documentation with author and decision points

operations.insert:
/* Documentation final override: author, decision points, less generic wording */
    function technicalDocSections() {
      return [
        { type: "title", text: "Pallet Check Tool" },
        { type: "subtitle", text: "Technical Documentation and Maintenance Guide" },
        { type: "meta", text: "Author: Krzysztof Machalica | Scope: standalone Pallet_Check_Tool.html | Audience: maintainers, process owners and technically capable users responsible for controlled changes." },

        { type: "page" },
        { type: "h1", text: "1. Document purpose" },
        { type: "p", text: "This document explains how the Pallet Check Tool is structured, which parts of the code control operational behaviour, and where future maintainers should make controlled changes. It is not an operator quick guide. It is a maintenance reference for people who need to understand the standalone HTML file before changing it." },
        { type: "p", text: "The main design constraint is distribution as one HTML file. The tool must continue to work when the file is copied, sent by e-mail or stored on a shared drive without any additional assets. For this reason, import logic, UI, state handling, documentation view and EAN mapping all live inside the same file." },
        { type: "p", text: "The document focuses on decision points: functions, constants and UI sections where a process owner may reasonably decide to change behaviour. Pure implementation details that should not normally be changed are mentioned only when they affect maintenance risk." },

        { type: "h1", text: "2. Application scope" },
        { type: "p", text: "The tool verifies whether the physical content of a pallet matches imported system data. The operator imports a batch, scans or types a Pallet ID, and then scans SKU or EAN codes found on the pallet. The tool tracks progress, highlights confirmed rows, records exceptions and stores completed pallet history locally." },
        { type: "p", text: "The tool does not connect to WMS directly, does not share data between users, does not store data centrally and does not validate against a live database. These are intentional boundaries. Any change that introduces online data, shared storage or server logic changes the operating model and should be treated as a separate project decision." },

        { type: "page" },
        { type: "h1", text: "3. Standalone HTML architecture" },
        { type: "p", text: "Pallet_Check_Tool.html contains the full application. The HTML section defines visible panels and controls. The CSS section defines layout, colours, table styling, top navigation, sticky statistics, documentation print view and responsive behaviour. The JavaScript section defines state, import, search, scan workflow, logs, history, exports, documentation generation and event binding." },
        { type: "p", text: "Because everything is in one file, changes should be small and isolated. A syntax error in JavaScript can prevent the entire tool from starting. A changed element ID can break event binding. A table column change can break both rendering and CSS selectors." },
        { type: "code", text: "Useful search terms inside the file:\nDEFAULT_MAPPING\nDEFAULT_STATE\nloadState\nsaveState\nparseRawData\nappendBatch\nbuildRowKey\nsearchPallet\nrenderResults\nrenderRowTable\nstartScanWorkflow\nhandleSkuScan\nresolveSkuOrEan\naddWrongScan\nlogCompletedPallet\nconfigureTechnicalPdfDownload" },

        { type: "h1", text: "4. State and persistence decision points" },
        { type: "p", text: "DEFAULT_STATE is the starting point for local application state. It defines what the tool knows before any user imports data. Fields such as rows, batchCount, mapping, completedHistory, palletActivity, wrongScans, scanMode, manualScanEntry, manualPalletEntry, scanLock, soundEnabled, customEanMap and lastImportDate are decision-sensitive because they affect the operating process." },
        { type: "p", text: "STORAGE_KEY controls where browser data is stored. Changing it forces the browser to start with a new local state. This can be useful after a breaking change, but it will also make previous local data appear missing. Do not change STORAGE_KEY for cosmetic releases." },
        { type: "p", text: "loadState() and saveState() are the persistence boundary. They should remain simple. Process logic should not be hidden inside persistence functions. When adding a new persistent setting, add it to DEFAULT_STATE and verify that saveState and loadState preserve it after refresh." },

        { type: "page" },
        { type: "h1", text: "5. Import and column mapping" },
        { type: "p", text: "DEFAULT_MAPPING defines the default source columns for quantity, SKU, Pallet ID and Container ID. These values are one-based because they are intended to match how users count spreadsheet columns. getColumnIndexes() converts them into zero-based JavaScript indexes." },
        { type: "p", text: "parseRawData(rawText) converts pasted system data into normalized records. This is the correct place to change accepted separators, skip rules, required fields or source-format assumptions. It should output clean records with sku, qty, palletId and containerId." },
        { type: "p", text: "appendBatch(rows, sourceLabel) applies the import business rules after parsing. It validates rows, removes duplicates, appends unique records, updates lastImportDate and saves state. The function intentionally separates parsing from acceptance. Keep it that way: parsing determines what the data means, appendBatch determines whether the data should be accepted." },
        { type: "p", text: "buildRowKey(row) is the duplicate decision point. The current key uses SKU, quantity, Pallet ID and Container ID. If operations decide that duplicates should be detected by another combination, change buildRowKey rather than spreading duplicate rules across the code." },
        { type: "code", text: "Import functions to review before changing source data rules:\nDEFAULT_MAPPING\ngetColumnIndexes()\nparseRawData(rawText)\nappendBatch(rows, sourceLabel)\nbuildRowKey(row)\nupdateImportStatus()" },

        { type: "h1", text: "6. Daily import warning" },
        { type: "p", text: "todayKey(), hasImportedToday() and lastImportDate support the warning shown when a pallet is not found and today's batch has not been imported. This is an operational safeguard. It prevents stale local data from being interpreted as an operator scanning error." },
        { type: "p", text: "If the business decides that old data is acceptable across shifts or days, this logic can be relaxed. If daily batch import is mandatory, keep the warning strict. The wording should remain clear and action-oriented." },

        { type: "page" },
        { type: "h1", text: "7. Pallet lookup and match handling" },
        { type: "p", text: "searchPallet() is one of the most important functions in the tool. It controls what happens after a user scans or enters a Pallet ID. It checks whether data exists, prevents switching away from unfinished work, handles suffix search, searches Pallet ID, checks Container ID matches and decides which warning or result state to show." },
        { type: "p", text: "The main process decision in searchPallet() is how strict the tool should be. A strict process blocks ambiguous actions and logs wrong scans. A more forgiving process may show warnings and allow the operator to continue. Any change here affects shop-floor behaviour and should be tested with real scanning scenarios." },
        { type: "p", text: "Container ID handling should be changed carefully. A container match may indicate that the operator scanned the wrong label. The tool should make that visible instead of silently accepting the value as a Pallet ID unless operations explicitly decide otherwise." },
        { type: "code", text: "Lookup functions and related decisions:\nsearchPallet() - primary lookup flow\nhasImportedToday() - stale data warning\nrenderResults() - result or empty state\naddWrongScan() - exception logging\nqueueFocusActiveInput() - scanner-ready focus after lookup" },

        { type: "h1", text: "8. Result rendering and table decisions" },
        { type: "p", text: "renderResults(rows, palletId, options) controls the full result area. It is the correct place to adjust empty-state behaviour, result labels and what happens when no rows are available. renderCurrentMeta(rows, palletId, labelText) controls summary calculations displayed next to the table." },
        { type: "p", text: "renderRowTable(rows) controls the visible SKU rows. This function should be reviewed before changing columns, row order, row highlights, displayed EAN, progress text or table structure. If a column is added or removed, update the HTML table header, renderRowTable and related nth-child CSS selectors together." },
        { type: "p", text: "The table is intentionally styled as a light operational list rather than a spreadsheet. If a maintainer reintroduces heavy borders or dense headers, readability during scanning may suffer." },

        { type: "page" },
        { type: "h1", text: "9. Scan workflow" },
        { type: "p", text: "startScanWorkflow(rows, palletId) starts verification for the active pallet. It creates requirements, resets counters and prepares the SKU/EAN input. This function is the correct place to change how scan requirements are built from imported data." },
        { type: "p", text: "handleSkuScan() is the central scan-processing function. It normalizes input, resolves EAN to SKU, checks whether the item belongs to the active pallet, updates counts, detects duplicates, logs invalid scans, plays sounds and checks whether the pallet is complete. This is a high-risk function because it combines process logic and operator feedback." },
        { type: "p", text: "evaluatePalletStatus(), updateScanStatus(), sumRequirementCounts() and sumScannedCounts() define completion state and progress. If completion rules change, update these functions as a group. Do not change only the UI text while leaving the underlying counters unchanged." },
        { type: "code", text: "Scan workflow functions:\nstartScanWorkflow(rows, palletId)\nhandleSkuScan()\nupdateScanStatus()\nevaluatePalletStatus()\nsumRequirementCounts()\nsumScannedCounts()\nupdateScanInstructionAndProgress()" },

        { type: "h1", text: "10. Completion modes" },
        { type: "p", text: "scanMode controls how the pallet is completed. Complete by SKU requires confirmation of each unique SKU. Complete by Quantity requires confirmation based on imported quantity. The mode affects requirements, progress text, row fill and completion detection." },
        { type: "p", text: "Adding a new mode is a product decision, not a small UI change. It requires updates to the Advanced Tools selector, DEFAULT_STATE, startScanWorkflow(), evaluatePalletStatus(), progress wording, row display, completed history wording and tests." },

        { type: "page" },
        { type: "h1", text: "11. EAN and SKU resolution" },
        { type: "p", text: "resolveSkuOrEan(value) is the function that decides whether scanned input should be interpreted as EAN or SKU. It checks lookup maps, supports shortened EAN handling and falls back to raw SKU when no mapping exists. This function is decision-sensitive because it defines what the scanner is allowed to accept." },
        { type: "p", text: "rebuildEanMaps() prepares lookup maps from the embedded EAN database and any custom uploaded map. parseEanCsv(rawText) controls how custom EAN data is read. reloadEanDatabase() refreshes the active database. stripEanPrefix(ean) handles the operational prefix-removal rule." },
        { type: "p", text: "If labels, supplier EANs or scanner output formats change, review EAN resolution before changing validation rules. Incorrect EAN logic can cause valid products to be rejected or incorrect products to be accepted." },
        { type: "code", text: "EAN/SKU functions:\nrebuildEanMaps()\nresolveSkuOrEan(value)\nstripEanPrefix(ean)\nparseEanCsv(rawText)\nreloadEanDatabase()\ngetSkuDisplayEan(sku)" },

        { type: "h1", text: "12. Wrong scans and exception policy" },
        { type: "p", text: "addWrongScan(type, value, details) records exceptions. This function reflects process policy: what should be treated as a wrong scan, what should be treated as a warning and what should be ignored. Do not add new exception types without checking how they will be interpreted by supervisors or process owners." },
        { type: "p", text: "renderWrongScanLog() displays the log. renderWrongScanSummary() calculates the summary. ACTIVITY_PAGE_SIZE controls pagination. The log is currently capped to keep localStorage and the UI manageable. If longer retention is required, discuss whether a local browser store is still appropriate." },

        { type: "page" },
        { type: "h1", text: "13. Completed pallet history" },
        { type: "p", text: "logCompletedPallet() records a completed pallet only when the workflow reaches completion. This avoids counting partially checked pallets as done. renderCompletedHistory() shows completed runs with pagination. exportCompletedHistoryCsv() provides a simple CSV output." },
        { type: "p", text: "The history cap is a decision point. Increasing it keeps more local records but can make the UI slower and localStorage larger. Reducing it keeps the tool lighter but may remove useful operational traceability." },

        { type: "h1", text: "14. Focus behaviour and scanner operation" },
        { type: "p", text: "getActiveInput(), queueFocusActiveInput() and queueFocusSkuInput() keep the workflow scanner-ready. This is important because barcode scanners usually behave like keyboards. The tool should return focus to the correct input after valid scans, warnings and modal actions." },
        { type: "p", text: "Manual modes intentionally reduce automatic focus and submit behaviour. This allows a user to type values, correct entries and interact with logs without the tool immediately stealing focus. Always test both scanner mode and manual mode after focus changes." },

        { type: "page" },
        { type: "h1", text: "15. UI and visual behaviour" },
        { type: "p", text: "Top navigation is controlled by side-tab-panel and tab-btn styles. Instructions, Settings and Data Import are shown as top-left sticky controls. Result statistics are controlled by meta-grid and meta-card. The result table is controlled by table-wrap, table selectors and row progress classes." },
        { type: "p", text: "The UI should stay operational and quiet. Avoid large decorative containers in instructions, excessive shadows, generic callout blocks or language that sounds promotional. The tool is used during a process, so speed, clarity and predictability are more important than visual effects." },
        { type: "p", text: "Documentation UI is controlled by doc-download-card, doc-download-link, buildTechnicalDocumentationPrintHtml() and configureTechnicalPdfDownload(). The documentation is opened in a separate printable view so the main application remains unaffected." },

        { type: "h1", text: "16. Messages, modals and sounds" },
        { type: "p", text: "setStatus(message, tone) controls inline feedback. Tone should match process severity: success for accepted work, warn for recoverable issues, error for blocked actions and info for neutral states. showWorkflowModal() controls larger confirmation and warning popups. beep(type) controls audio feedback." },
        { type: "p", text: "Message text should be direct and operational. Avoid vague wording. A good message tells the user what happened and what to do next." },

        { type: "page" },
        { type: "h1", text: "17. Exports and download functions" },
        { type: "p", text: "exportCsv(filename, rows, headers) is the shared CSV export utility. exportCurrentView() exports the active pallet view. exportCompletedHistoryCsv() exports completed pallet history. New exports should reuse exportCsv rather than creating separate download logic." },
        { type: "p", text: "Documentation output is handled separately. buildTechnicalDocumentationPrintHtml() creates a printable maintenance guide. downloadTechnicalPdf(event) opens the documentation view. configureTechnicalPdfDownload() wires the button in Instructions. The name still refers to PDF because the expected user output is Save as PDF, but the stable implementation uses browser print rather than manual PDF byte generation." },

        { type: "h1", text: "18. Clear, reset and destructive actions" },
        { type: "p", text: "Clear and reset actions are process-sensitive because they affect locally stored imported data, logs and history. Confirmation should remain in place for destructive actions. If a future version separates operational data from settings, reset logic should also be separated so users can clear data without losing preferred configuration." },

        { type: "page" },
        { type: "h1", text: "19. Decision-point checklist" },
        { type: "p", text: "Before changing import logic, decide whether the source export format changed permanently or only one file is unusual. Before changing duplicate detection, define which fields make a row unique. Before changing pallet lookup, decide how strict the process should be with suffixes, container labels and stale data." },
        { type: "p", text: "Before changing scan completion, decide whether the business wants confirmation by SKU, by quantity or by another operational rule. Before changing EAN handling, confirm scanner output format and supplier label rules. Before changing logs, decide what supervisors need to review later. Before changing localStorage, decide whether old local data should survive the release." },
        { type: "p", text: "Before changing UI, check whether the change improves scanning speed and clarity. Before changing messages, check whether the user can understand the next action without asking a supervisor. Before changing documentation, test the tool as a single copied HTML file." },

        { type: "h1", text: "20. Test protocol" },
        { type: "p", text: "For every functional change, refresh with Ctrl+F5, clear local data, import a known batch, search an existing pallet, scan a valid SKU or EAN, scan an invalid value, trigger a container-related warning if possible, complete a pallet, check completed history, check wrong scan log, refresh again and confirm persistence." },
        { type: "p", text: "For every distribution change, copy only the HTML file to another location and test it there. The tool, documentation and downloads must not depend on repository files, external folders or internet access." },

        { type: "page" },
        { type: "h1", text: "21. Troubleshooting guide" },
        { type: "p", text: "If the page does not start, check the browser console for JavaScript syntax errors. If import returns no rows, check DEFAULT_MAPPING and parseRawData(). If a pallet is not found, confirm the current batch was imported and that the scanned value is actually a Pallet ID. If EAN is rejected, check resolveSkuOrEan(), stripEanPrefix() and the active map." },
        { type: "p", text: "If the table layout looks wrong after a change, check renderRowTable() and nth-child CSS selectors. If buttons stop working, check element IDs and bindEvents(). If documentation does not open, check browser pop-up settings. If local data appears missing after release, check whether STORAGE_KEY was changed." },

        { type: "h1", text: "22. Mental model" },
        { type: "p", text: "The safest way to understand the tool is to follow the data flow: import data into state.rows, search pallet into currentView.rows, start scan into scanState.requirements, scan SKU or EAN into scanState.scannedCounts, render progress from scanState, complete pallet into completedHistory, record exceptions into wrongScans and persist everything through localStorage." },
        { type: "p", text: "Most reliable changes respect this flow. Most risky changes bypass it, duplicate logic in multiple places or adjust UI without updating the underlying state." }
      ];
    }

    function buildTechnicalDocumentationPrintHtml() {
      const sections = technicalDocSections();
      const rows = sections.map((item) => {
        if (item.type === "page") return '<div class="page-break"></div>';
        if (item.type === "title") return '<h1 class="doc-title">' + escapeHtml(item.text) + '</h1>';
        if (item.type === "subtitle") return '<div class="subtitle">' + escapeHtml(item.text) + '</div>';
        if (item.type === "meta") return '<div class="meta">' + escapeHtml(item.text) + '</div>';
        if (item.type === "h1") return '<h2>' + escapeHtml(item.text) + '</h2>';
        if (item.type === "code") return '<pre>' + escapeHtml(item.text) + '</pre>';
        return '<p>' + escapeHtml(item.text) + '</p>';
      }).join("");

      return '<!doctype html><html><head><meta charset="utf-8"><title>Pallet Check Tool - Technical Documentation</title>' +
        '<style>' +
        '@page{size:A4;margin:18mm 18mm 16mm 18mm;}' +
        'body{font-family:Arial,Helvetica,sans-serif;color:#162435;background:#f6f8fb;line-height:1.48;font-size:10.5pt;margin:0;}' +
        '.toolbar{position:sticky;top:0;background:#0f2138;color:#fff;padding:12px 18px;display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:center;box-shadow:0 8px 20px rgba(8,20,34,.16);}' +
        '.toolbar span{font-weight:700;font-size:13px;}.toolbar button{border:1px solid rgba(255,255,255,.24);background:#0b5cab;color:#fff;border-radius:8px;padding:8px 12px;font-weight:700;cursor:pointer;}' +
        '.page{max-width:790px;margin:28px auto 42px;background:#fff;padding:42px 48px;box-shadow:0 16px 42px rgba(8,20,34,.10);}' +
        '.eyebrow{font-size:8.5pt;letter-spacing:.08em;text-transform:uppercase;color:#5d7084;margin-bottom:8px;}' +
        '.doc-title{font-size:28pt;line-height:1.08;margin:0 0 8px;color:#0b3157;}' +
        '.subtitle{font-size:14pt;color:#0b5cab;font-weight:700;margin:0 0 10px;}' +
        '.meta{font-size:9.5pt;color:#5d7084;border-top:1px solid #d8e1ea;border-bottom:1px solid #d8e1ea;padding:12px 0;margin:18px 0 24px;}' +
        'h2{font-size:15pt;line-height:1.25;color:#0b5cab;margin:20px 0 7px;page-break-after:avoid;}' +
        'p{margin:0 0 9px;}pre{font-family:Consolas,Monaco,monospace;font-size:8.5pt;line-height:1.35;background:#f0f4f8;border:1px solid #d8e1ea;border-radius:8px;padding:10px 12px;white-space:pre-wrap;}' +
        '.page-break{break-before:page;page-break-before:always;height:1px;}' +
        '.footer{margin-top:28px;padding-top:10px;border-top:1px solid #d8e1ea;color:#6b7c8e;font-size:8.5pt;}' +
        '@media print{body{background:#fff;font-size:10pt;}.toolbar{display:none!important;}.page{box-shadow:none;margin:0;max-width:none;padding:0;}.page-break{break-before:page;page-break-before:always;}h2{break-after:avoid;page-break-after:avoid;}pre{break-inside:avoid;page-break-inside:avoid;}}' +
        '</style></head><body><div class="toolbar"><span>Pallet Check Tool - Technical Documentation</span><button onclick="window.print()">Print / Save as PDF</button></div>' +
        '<main class="page"><div class="eyebrow">Standalone HTML maintenance guide</div>' + rows +
        '<div class="footer">Author: Krzysztof Machalica. Generated from Pallet_Check_Tool.html. This documentation is embedded in the standalone tool file.</div></main>' +
        '</body></html>';
    }

    function downloadTechnicalPdf(event) {
      if (event) event.preventDefault();
      const printWindow = window.open("", "_blank");
      if (!printWindow) {
        alert("Please allow pop-ups for this page to open the documentation view.");
        return;
      }
      printWindow.document.open();
      printWindow.document.write(buildTechnicalDocumentationPrintHtml());
      printWindow.document.close();
    }

    function configureTechnicalPdfDownload() {
      const link = document.querySelector(".doc-download-card .doc-download-link");
      if (!link) return;
      link.setAttribute("href", "#");
      link.removeAttribute("download");
      link.textContent = "Open documentation";
      link.onclick = downloadTechnicalPdf;
    }

operations.old: Reference for maintainers: file structure, import parsing, scan workflow, local storage, EAN mapping and safe change points. Opens a full maintenance guide generated from this HTML file. Use the document toolbar to print or save as PDF.
operations.new: Reference for maintainers and process owners. Covers file structure, decision points, import rules, scan workflow, local storage, EAN mapping, UI behaviour and safe change control.
